query: Japan Fiscal 2026 Supplementary Budget provisions Middle East economic impact Takaichi press remarks

mako.raw:
{"query":"Japan Fiscal 2026 Supplementary Budget provisions Middle East economic impact Takaichi press remarks","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1633,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://japan.kantei.go.jp/105/speech/202605/25kaiken.html","title":"Prime Minister Takaichi Spoke to the Press Regarding the Fiscal 2026 Supplementary Budget Taking into Account the Situation in the Middle East | Speeches | Prime Minister's Office of Japan","content":"We are currently continuing our subsidies for gasoline, diesel oil, heavy oil, kerosene, and so on through the use of fiscal 2025's reserve funds.
Looking at crude oil, our alternative sourcing for June that does not traverse the Strait of Hormuz was forecast to surpass 70 percent as of May 12.
Even in that instance, we will be able to secure a stable supply of petroleum beyond the end of fiscal 2026 [(March 2027)] until spring 2027.
At the same time, it appears that 3 trillion yen worth of deficit-financing bonds for the previous fiscal year, fiscal 2025, do not in fact need to be issued."},{"url":"https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/japan-pm-sees-no-need-now-compile-extra-budget-2026-04-27/","title":"Japan PM sees no need for now to compile extra budget | Reuters","content":"TOKYO, April 27 (Reuters) - Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said ​on Monday she did ‌not see a need "for now" to compile a ​supplementary budget aimed ​at cushioning the economic blow ⁠from the Middle ​East conflict.
Speaking in parliament, Takaichi ​also said the government should not take steps to ​curb economic activity, despite ​concern that Japan may face ‌shortages ⁠in crude oil and oil-related products due to the effective closure of ​the ​Strait ⁠of Hormuz."},{"url":"https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-18/japan-s-takaichi-set-to-announce-extra-budget-amid-mideast-shock","title":"Japan’s Takaichi U-Turns on Extra Budget After Mideast Shock - Bloomberg","content":"Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi called for an extra budget in response to rising commodity prices driven by the ongoing Middle East conflict, backtracking on her insistence that such a measure wasn’t necessary and helping spark fresh record highs in super-long bond yields."},{"url":"https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2026/06/03/japan-extra-budget-mideast-impact/","title":"Japan unveils ¥3.1 trillion extra budget to cushion Mideast impact - The Japan Times","content":"Japan unveils ¥3.1 trillion extra budget to cushion Mideast impact
Japan’s Cabinet has approved a ¥3.1 trillion ($19.4 billion) extra budget to fund measures meant to cushion households from inflation tied to Middle East turbulence, putting fiscal policy back in the spotlight for bond investors.
Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s Cabinet has endorsed a ¥3.1 trillion package that includes a newly created ¥2.5 trillion reserve fund to respond to rising prices for commodities by providing subsidies."},{"url":"https://japan.kantei.go.jp/105/speech/202604/07kaiken.html","title":"Press Conference by Prime Minister Takaichi regarding the Passage of the FY2026 Budget and Responses to the Situation in the Middle East | Speeches | Prime Minister's Office of Japan","content":"Press Conference by Prime Minister Takaichi regarding the Passage of the FY2026 Budget and Responses to the Situation in the Middle East
As a result, the total amount of the general account budget has reached a record high of 122.3 trillion yen.
With the passage of the FY2026 budget today, the reserve fund included in the budget can now be utilized if necessary.
In particular, with regard to medical care, we established a task force at the end of last month headed by the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare and the Minister of Economy, Trade and Industry, and have been advancing our response.
As for whether we will request energy conservation or demand restraint from the public, it is important to note that, as a resource-poor country, Japan has traditionally called for energy-saving efforts during periods of peak demand in summer and winter.
Therefore, as of now, the government does not consider it necessary to immediately compile a supplementary budget.
With regard to President Trump’s remarks, I will not comment on each individual statement."}]}

tavily.raw: {"query":"Japan Fiscal 2026 Supplementary Budget provisions Middle East economic impact Takaichi press remarks","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://japan.kantei.go.jp/105/speech/202605/25kaiken.html","title":"Prime Minister Takaichi Spoke to the Press Regarding ...","content":"Prime Minister Takaichi Spoke to the Press Regarding the Fiscal 2026 Supplementary Budget Taking into Account the Situation in the Middle East.","score":0.8690161,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2026/06/03/japan-extra-budget-mideast-impact","title":"Japan unveils ¥3.1 trillion extra budget to cushion Mideast impact","content":"Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s Cabinet has endorsed a ¥3.1 trillion extra budget to fund measures meant to cushion households from inflation tied to Middle East turbulence. Image 10: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s Cabinet has endorsed a ¥3.1 trillion extra budget to fund measures meant to cushion households from inflation tied to Middle East turbulence. Japan’s Cabinet has approved a¥3.1 trillion ($19.4 billion) extra budget to fund measures meant to cushion households from inflation tied to Middle East turbulence, putting fiscal policy back in the spotlight for bond investors. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s Cabinet has endorsed a ¥3.1 trillion package that includes a newly created ¥2.5 trillion reserve fund to respond to rising prices for commodities by providing subsidies. While it’s common for Japan to compile extra budgets to cover unexpected costs from natural disasters or provide economic stimulus at some point in the fiscal year, the need for a supplementary budget barely a month after parliament approved the annual budget may heighten concerns about the spending trajectory.","score":0.84858394,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://japan.kantei.go.jp/105/speech/202604/07kaiken.html","title":"Press Conference by Prime Minister Takaichi regarding the Passage of the FY2026 Budget and Responses to the Situation in the Middle East | Speeches | Prime Minister's Office of Japan","content":"# Press Conference by Prime Minister Takaichi regarding the Passage of the FY2026 Budget and Responses to the Situation in the Middle East. While it is regrettable that the budget could not be passed within the fiscal year, we have, with the understanding that “everything is for the peace of mind of the people and the building of a strong economy,” sought the cooperation of all those involved and benefited from their dedicated efforts. As a result, by responding sincerely to deliberations in the Diet, we believe we have been able to minimize, to the greatest extent possible, the risk of disruption to people’s daily lives. The FY2026 budget passed today is based on the concept of “responsible and proactive public finances.” While making strategic choices across the budget as a whole, it provides for bold increases in areas such as strategic investments that enhance resilience against potential crises and investments that promote growth, thereby contributing to the realization of a “strong economy.”.","score":0.8286204,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20260518/p2g/00m/0na/022000c","title":"Japan PM looks to mull extra budget for FY 2026 to ease oil price impact - The Mainichi","content":"TOKYO (Kyodo) -- Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said Monday her government will consider compiling an extra budget for fiscal 2026 to ease the impact of elevated crude oil prices, fueling renewed concerns about Japan's deteriorating finances as long-term bond yields move at nearly 30-year highs. The size of the supplementary budget has come under scrutiny after a record 122.31 trillion yen ($770 billion) budget was enacted for the year starting April, underscoring Japan's heavy reliance on debt and reflecting Takaichi's stance of pursuing expansionary spending to spur economic growth. Given surging crude oil prices, the government has set aside over 1 trillion yen for the gasoline price subsidies, restarted in mid-March, including reserve funds in the fiscal 2025 budget. The advance crafting of the extra budget comes as the yields on the benchmark 10-year Japanese government bond have been trending higher amid persistent concerns over Japan's fiscal sustainability under the Takaichi government.","score":0.7803451,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.bssnews.net/business/392747","title":"Japan passes $19 bn extra budget to ease Middle East war ...","content":"The budget had been approved by Takaichi's cabinet on Tuesday. She has said the money will go towards easing petrol, electricity and gas costs.","score":0.6519982,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.9,"request_id":"deb7e5c8-d15e-45d9-bfdb-d857de2ce7a1"}
